title:
Velocitas vs Artio vs QuickFIX/J — I built the same FIX order gateway pipeline
   in all three and measured it
author: Potential-Access-595
selftext:
Most FIX benchmarks measure parse() in a tight loop. I wanted real numbers, so

   I built the same nine-step pipeline three times — TCP in → parse → Aeron IPC

  → executor → Aeron back → serialize → TCP out — once in Rust (Velocitas), once

   in Java (Artio), once in Java (QuickFIX/J). Same workload, 100k round-trips ×

   5 runs.



  Medians:



  \- Velocitas (Rust): p50 20 µs, p99.9 114 µs, 41,720 RT/s

  \- QuickFIX/J: p50 51 µs, p99.9 221 µs, 17,887 RT/s

  \- Artio: p50 79 µs, p99.9 181 µs, 11,986 RT/s — but best max of the three (940

   µs)



  The Artio story turned out more interesting than the win. Its engine/library

  split costs \~30 µs at p50 but gives it the smoothest tail. QFJ's 4 ms max is a

   GC pause. Rust wins everywhere except worst-case max, where scheduler jitter

  on busy-spin threads bites.



  Also: my first Artio run was slower than QFJ until I killed allocation on the

  hot path (22× tail improvement from fixing new String(...) and String.format).

   Java benchmarks punish anyone who doesn't audit every method.



  Full write-up with methodology, tuning notes, and reproduction commands:

  \[link\]



  Repo (MIT): [https://github.com/matthart1983/velocitas-fix-engine](https://github.com/matthart1983/velocitas-fix-engine)
